title: Variables
description: Meet x as a name for an unknown amount you already know how to find.

scaffolded.title: Meet x
scaffolded.body: You already know how to find a mystery block. In algebra, we just name it **x**.

steps.text: A lone mystery block balances 7 pounds. We name it x.

gate.template: The letter x is just a ___ for the unknown amount.
gate.hint: We use the letter x as a stand-in label for an amount we don't know yet, not as a weight or a fixed number.

steps.text: x sits all by itself, nothing added.

gate.template: Since x sits ___, it equals the other pan.
gate.hint: Nothing else is sitting on x's pan, so x by itself has to match whatever the other pan holds.

steps.text: Read the balanced scale to find x.

gate.hint: x is alone on its pan, so just read the weight resting on the other pan to see what x must equal.

scaffolded.prompt: **x** stands for a mystery amount. If x balances 6 pounds, what is x?
hints: The letter x is just a name for the block's weight.
x sits alone on its pan, so it equals whatever the other pan holds.
Read the weight resting across from x to name its value.

feedback.correct: Exactly! x sits alone, so it's worth whatever balances it, 6 pounds.
incorrect: Not quite. x is alone, so it equals the weight across from it.
Look at the right side of the scale.

scaffolded.title: When x has extra weight
scaffolded.body: Often x has a known weight sitting with it. Take that weight off both pans to free x.

interaction.resultCaption: Take 3 off each side: 11 becomes 8, so x balances 8 alone.

predict.prompt: Predict: take 3 off each pan and the right pan reads...

scaffolded.prompt: x plus 2 pounds balances 8 pounds. Get x by itself, keep both pans balanced!

feedback.correct: Nice, you took 2 off both sides to keep it fair, and x is 6.
incorrect: After removing 2 from 8, what's left on the right?
x is alone now. It equals the remaining weight.

hints: A number sitting with x is just riding along, not part of x.
Take the 2 pounds off both pans so x is left by itself.
With the 2 gone, read what x balances on its own.

feedback.correct: Both pans stayed equal the whole way, so x = 6.
incorrect: That changes only one side. Keep both sides equal.
Remove 2 from both pans, then read what x balances.

scaffolded.prompt: If x + 1 = 5, what does x equal?
hints: To free x, undo whatever is added to it.
Take 1 off both sides of x + 1 = 5 to get x alone.
After removing 1 from each side, see what x is left equal to.

feedback.correct: Yes! You undid the +1, so x = 4.
incorrect: x plus 1 makes 5, so take that extra 1 back off.

scaffolded.prompt: Two x blocks balance 10 pounds. What is one x block?
hints: Every copy of x weighs exactly the same.
Split the 10 pounds evenly between the two matching x blocks.
Think what number, used twice, would balance 10.

feedback.correct: Exactly! Two equal x blocks split 10, so each is 5.
incorrect: Split 10 equally between the two matching x blocks.

mastery.prompt: If x balances 9 pounds, x equals...

feedback.correct: Yes! x is on its own, so it's just what it balances, 9.
incorrect: x is alone, so read the weight across from it.

feedback.correct: Right, clear the 3 first, and x = 7.

followUp.prompt: What if it were x + 6 = 10 instead? Would x be bigger or smaller?
followUp.why: Taking a bigger number off both sides leaves less for x, so x is smaller.

mastery.prompt: Two x blocks balance 14 pounds. One x equals...

feedback.correct: Exactly, two equal x's share 14, so each is 7.
incorrect: Two matching x blocks share 14 equally.

followUp.prompt: What if two x blocks balanced 18 instead of 14? Would one x be bigger or smaller?
followUp.why: A bigger total split between the same two x blocks gives each one more, so x is bigger.
